tagline: Calculate gallons, kits, primer, and cost for epoxy floor coatings
title: Epoxy Floor Calculator — Gallons, Kits & Cost Estimate
metaDescription: Calculate epoxy floor coating materials: gallons, kits, primer, and cost. Covers thin coat, self-leveling, and decorative flake systems.
keywords: epoxy floor calculator, epoxy garage floor, epoxy coating calculator, how much epoxy do I need, epoxy floor cost
ogTitle: Epoxy Floor Calculator — Gallons, Kits & Cost Estimate

jsonLdWeb.name: Epoxy Floor Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate gallons, kits, primer, and cost for epoxy floor coating projects.

mainEntity.name: How much epoxy do I need for a garage floor?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 2-car garage (400-500 sq ft) needs about 1.5-2 gallons of thin-coat epoxy per coat, or 2 gallons of primer plus 2-3 gallons of color coat. Most kits cover 200-250 sq ft per kit. For a 2-car garage, plan on 2 kits.

mainEntity.name: What is the coverage rate for epoxy floor coating?

acceptedAnswer.text: Thin-coat epoxy covers 200-400 sq ft per gallon (depending on porosity). Self-leveling epoxy covers 12-16 sq ft per gallon at 1/8" thickness. Decorative flake broadcast coats cover 200-300 sq ft per gallon for the base coat.

mainEntity.name: Do I need primer for an epoxy floor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Primer is recommended for most floors, especially porous concrete. A primer coat improves adhesion and reduces pinholes. Primer covers 250-350 sq ft per gallon. On well-prepared, non-porous concrete, some systems allow skipping primer.

mainEntity.name: How many coats of epoxy do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Minimum 2 coats for most projects: 1 primer/base coat + 1 color coat. For high-traffic areas, add a clear topcoat (3 coats total). Self-leveling systems typically need 1 coat at 1/8" thickness. Decorative flake systems use 3 coats: primer, flake broadcast, and clear topcoat.

mainEntity.name: How much does epoxy flooring cost per square foot?

acceptedAnswer.text: DIY epoxy kits cost $1.50-$3.00 per sq ft for materials. Professional installation runs $3-$7 per sq ft for standard coatings and $7-$12 for decorative or self-leveling systems. A 2-car garage costs $600-$1,500 DIY or $1,500-$3,500 installed.

Self-leveling epoxy is a thick, pourable coating (1/8" to 1/4") that flows and levels itself for a smooth, seamless finish. It covers only 12-16 sq ft per gallon (much less than thin coats) but creates a durable, high-build floor. It is common in commercial kitchens, labs, and showrooms.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Epoxy Floor Materials

Divide floor area by the coverage rate for your coating type, then multiply by number of coats. Add primer separately.

Gallons = (Area ÷ Coverage Rate) × Number of Coats
  • Thin coat: 200–400 sq ft/gal (use 300 as average)
  • Self-leveling: 12–16 sq ft/gal at 1/8" thick (use 14 avg)
  • Decorative flake: 200–300 sq ft/gal base (use 250 avg)
  • Primer: ~300 sq ft/gal, 1 coat recommended
  • Kits: typical kit covers ~250 sq ft
  • Porous concrete may reduce coverage by 20–30%
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How much epoxy for a garage floor?

A 2-car garage (400-500 sq ft) needs 1.5-2 gallons of thin coat per coat, or 2 kits. Add primer at 1.5-2 gallons. Self-leveling needs 28-36 gallons for the same area.

What coverage rate per gallon?

Thin coat: 200-400 sqft/gal. Self-leveling: 12-16 sqft/gal. Flake systems: 200-300 sqft/gal for base coat.

Do I need primer?

Recommended for most floors, especially porous concrete. Primer covers 250-350 sqft/gal and improves adhesion and reduces pinholes.

How many coats of epoxy?

Minimum 2: primer + color coat. For high traffic, add a clear topcoat (3 total). Self-leveling usually needs just 1 thick coat.

How much does epoxy flooring cost?

DIY: $1.50-$3.00/sq ft for thin coat, $4-$8 for self-leveling. Pro install: $3-$12/sq ft. A 2-car garage runs $600-$1,500 DIY.

What is self-leveling epoxy?

A thick pourable coating (1/8"-1/4") that flows to a smooth finish. Covers only 12-16 sqft/gal but creates a very durable, seamless floor.

howWeCalculate.intro: Epoxy coverage is governed by one number: theoretical coverage is 1,604 sq ft per gallon at 1 mil of dry film. Divide that by your target thickness in mils to get real-world sq ft per gallon, then scale by area and coat count.
steps: Take the target film thickness. A rolled thin coat lands around 5–10 mils; a garage color coat is often specified at 10–12 mils.
Divide 1,600 by the mils to get coverage per gallon. At 12 mils that is about 133 sq ft per gallon; at 8 mils, about 200.
Divide your floor area by that coverage number to get gallons for a single coat.
Multiply by the number of coats. A primer coat and a color coat is the common two-coat garage stack; add a clear topcoat for three.
Bump the total up 20–30% on bare, porous, or freshly ground concrete, which drinks the first coat before it films out.
howWeCalculate.disclaimer: Coverage numbers assume a properly prepped, non-porous surface and the mil thickness you actually roll to. Deep pits, control joints, and heavy profile from grinding all pull more product. Buy a little extra rather than run short mid-coat and leave a lap line.

whenToUse.intro: Reach for this before you buy kits, so you order enough gallons for every coat instead of guessing from the label's optimistic sq-ft-per-kit number.
scenarios: Coating a garage floor: a 2-car bay runs 400–500 sq ft, so at a 10–12 mil color coat plan on roughly 3 gallons per coat and 6 for a two-coat job.
Sealing a basement floor where you want a thinner 5–8 mil roll-on and need to know how many gallons that stretches to across the whole slab.
Prepping a workshop or shed floor and deciding whether one coat will hold up or whether traffic warrants a second coat plus a clear topcoat.
Comparing a thin roll-on coat against a self-leveling pour, where coverage drops from a few hundred sq ft per gallon to 12–16 sq ft per gallon.
Checking a contractor's material line item — if the gallons quoted do not cover your area at the stated mils and coat count, ask why.

sections.title: How Epoxy Coverage Really Works
sections.body: Every epoxy coating starts from the same physical fact: one gallon spread one mil thick covers about 1,604 square feet. Thickness is what changes everything. Roll that same gallon to a 12-mil garage finish and it only reaches around 133 square feet. Take a 400 sq ft garage at roughly 12 mils and you need about 3 gallons per coat — run the standard two coats and that is close to 6 gallons of product, before you add primer. This calculator turns your area, target mils, and coat count into a gallon figure so you order once instead of driving back for another kit.

sections.title: Thickness, Coats, and Why the Label Lies a Little
sections.body: Kit boxes advertise coverage at the thinnest film the product will form, so a box that says 500 sq ft is often assuming a single 5-mil pass. Most real garage jobs want more film than that: a primer coat to grip the concrete, then a color coat rolled to 10–12 mils for durability. Two coats roughly doubles your gallons, and bare or freshly ground concrete soaks up the first coat, cutting effective coverage 20–30%. That gap between the label number and the real number is exactly where people run short.

sections.title: Thin Coat vs. Self-Leveling
sections.body: There are two very different products behind the word epoxy. A rolled thin coat lands at 5–12 mils and covers a few hundred square feet per gallon — this is what most garages and workshops use. A self-leveling pour is applied at 1/8 to 1/4 inch, so a single gallon only covers 12–16 square feet. On the same 400 sq ft floor, a thin two-coat job might be 6 gallons while a 1/8-inch self-leveling floor is closer to 28–36 gallons. Pick the coating type in the calculator first, because it swings the total by an order of magnitude.

howToSteps.title: Enter your floor area
howToSteps.description: Type the square footage directly, or switch to length × width and enter the bay dimensions — a 22 × 20 garage is 440 sq ft. Measure to the wall, not the parked-car footprint.

howToSteps.title: Pick the coating type
howToSteps.description: Thin coat for a rolled garage or workshop finish, self-leveling for a poured 1/8-inch floor, or decorative flake for a chip-broadcast system. This sets the coverage rate the math uses.

howToSteps.title: Set the number of coats
howToSteps.description: Two coats is the norm for a garage: primer or base plus a color coat. Add a third for a clear topcoat over high-traffic or flaked floors. One coat is usually only enough for a light basement seal.

howToSteps.title: Read gallons, kits, and primer
howToSteps.description: The result shows total gallons for all coats, how many kits that is, and a separate primer figure. Add your price per gallon to see the material cost, and round gallons up so you finish every coat wet.

tips: Order 10–15% more than the calculator shows. Overlap at walls, refilling the roller tray, and pot life waste all eat into your gallons, and you cannot pause a coat to buy more.
Bare concrete is thirsty. A freshly acid-etched or ground slab can pull the first coat down to 70–80% of its rated coverage, so treat primer coverage as a range, not a promise.
Two thin coats beat one thick one. Epoxy rolled too heavy in a single pass traps solvent and can blush or peel — build your mils across coats instead.
Match kit chemistry to the job: water-based epoxy films thinner (5–8 mils) than 100%-solids products (10–15+ mils), so the same square footage needs different gallons.
Do not skip primer on porous or previously coated concrete. It anchors the color coat and stops pinholes from outgassing, and it is cheaper per gallon than the finish coat it protects.

faq.question: How much epoxy do I need per square foot?
faq.answer: It depends entirely on thickness. At the theoretical 1,604 sq ft per gallon at 1 mil, a 10-mil coat covers about 160 sq ft per gallon and a 12-mil coat about 133. So a single 10-mil coat runs roughly 1 gallon per 160 sq ft; a two-coat garage job is about double that.

faq.question: How does film thickness in mils change coverage?
faq.answer: Coverage is inversely proportional to mils. Start from 1,600 sq ft per gallon at 1 mil, then divide by your target: 5 mils gives ~320 sq ft/gal, 8 mils ~200, 12 mils ~133, 16 mils ~100. Thicker film means more durability but fewer square feet per gallon.

faq.question: Do I need one coat or two?
faq.answer: Most garage and workshop floors get two: a primer or base coat that bonds to the concrete, then a color coat rolled to full thickness. One coat is only reasonable for a light basement seal on tight concrete. High-traffic floors add a third clear topcoat for wear and chemical resistance.

faq.question: Do I need primer, or can I skip it?
faq.answer: Primer is strongly recommended on bare, porous, or acid-etched concrete. It penetrates and grips the slab, evens out absorption so your color coat films uniformly, and reduces pinholes from outgassing. You can sometimes skip it on dense, mechanically ground concrete with a 100%-solids self-priming system, but it is cheap insurance against peeling.

faq.question: How much epoxy for a 2-car garage?
faq.answer: A 2-car garage is typically 400–500 sq ft. For a rolled thin coat at 10–12 mils, plan on about 3 gallons per coat, so roughly 6 gallons for a standard two-coat job plus 1.5–2 gallons of primer. That usually works out to 2–3 kits depending on kit size.

faq.question: Why does the kit box cover more area than this calculator says?
faq.answer: Kit coverage on the box is quoted at the thinnest film the product will form, often a single 5-mil pass, and assumes smooth non-porous concrete. Real jobs use thicker coats, multiple coats, and porous slabs that absorb product, so plan for meaningfully less coverage than the label headline.
